items.mistake: Treating a PDF value f(x) as the probability that X equals x
items.fix: Use P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = ∫ f(x) dx; for continuous X, P(X = x) = 0. Compare densities only relative to a reference or over intervals.
items.why: Confusing density with probability leads to invalid statements like “P(X = 3) = 0.4” for a Normal model.
items.example: N(0,1) has f(0) ≈ 0.399, but P(X = 0) = 0; P(-0.5 ≤ X ≤ 0.5) ≈ 0.38 from the CDF or integral.

items.mistake: Using Binomial when trials are not independent or p varies per trial
items.fix: Use a hierarchical model, Beta-Binomial, or per-trial logistic regression instead of a single global p.
items.why: Binomial requires fixed n and identical independent Bernoulli trials; violated assumptions inflate false confidence.
items.example: Ten ad clicks from the same user are correlated; modeling them as Binomial(10, p) overstates effective sample size.

items.mistake: Applying Poisson to over-dispersed counts (variance much larger than mean)
items.fix: Check mean vs variance on training counts; consider Negative Binomial, zero-inflated Poisson, or compound models.
items.why: Poisson forces Var(X) = λ; extra variance indicates unmodeled heterogeneity or clustering.
items.example: Daily website hits with mean 50 but variance 500 should not be modeled as Poisson(50) without modification.

items.mistake: Using Gaussian Naive Bayes on skewed or bounded features without transformation
items.fix: Log-transform positive skewed features, winsorize outliers, or pick a different likelihood (e.g. Bernoulli for binary features).
items.why: GNB assumes unimodal symmetric Normal class-conditions; heavy skew makes μ and σ poor summaries.
items.example: Income in dollars per class is right-skewed; log(income) is often closer to Normal within class than raw income.
